The state prevents duplicate notifications while maintaining diagnostic information.\n6\t\n7\t## State Structure\n8\t\n9\t```python\n10\t@dataclass\n11\tclass FirstFailureState:\n12\t    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n13\t    \n14\t    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n15\t    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n16\t    \"\"\"\n17\t    # Has the first failure been recorded?\n18\t    has_failed: bool = False\n19\t    \n20\t    # When did the first failure occur?\n21\t    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n22\t    \n23\t    # Which channel failed?\n24\t    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n25\t    \n26\t    # What was the error?\n27\t    error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"TelegramError\", \"TimeoutError\"\n28\t    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n29\t    \n30\t    # Total failure count (for diagnostics)\n31\t    total_failures: int = 0\n32\t    \n33\t    # Have we sent the notification about this first failure?\n34\t    notification_sent: bool = False\n35\t    \n36\t    # When did we send the notification?\n37\t    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n38\t```\n39\t\n40\t## Field Descriptions\n41\t\n42\t### Core State\n43\t\n44\t- **`has_failed: bool`** — Sentinel flag indicating whether ANY failure has occurred since startup. This is the primary field checked before triggering \"first failure\" logic.\n45\t\n46\t- **`first_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of the first failure occurrence. Set once on the first failure, never updated. Use UTC timezone-aware datetimes.\n47\t\n48\t- **`channel_id: Optional[str]`** — The Telegram channel ID that failed on the first occurrence. Useful for diagnosing whether a specific channel is problematic.\n49\t\n50\t### Error Information\n51\t\n52\t- **`error_type: Optional[str]`** — Exception class name (e.g., `\"TelegramError\"`, `\"ConnectionError\"`). Helps categorize the failure type.\n53\t\n54\t- **`error_message: Optional[str]`** — The error message or summary. Useful for quick diagnosis without digging through logs.\n55\t\n56\t### Diagnostics\n57\t\n58\t- **`total_failures: int`** — Monotonically increasing counter of ALL failures since startup. Helps assess failure frequency even after the first failure notification.\n59\t\n60\t### Notification Tracking\n61\t\n62\t- **`notification_sent: bool`** — Prevents duplicate notifications. Set to `True` after the first-failure alert is sent.\n63\t\n64\t- **`notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of when the notification was sent. Useful for audit trails and debugging.\n65\t\n66\t## Reset Scenarios\n67\t\n68\t### 1. Application Startup (Automatic)\n69\t\n70\t```python\n71\t# At module load time in src/monitoring/ambient.py\n72\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n73\t```\n74\t\n75\tAll fields default to their initial values (fresh state).\n76\t\n77\t### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action)\n78\t\n79\tIf an admin manually resolves a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking:\n80\t\n81\t```python\n82\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # Fresh instance\n83\t```\n84\t\n85\tOr selectively:\n86\t\n87\t```python\n88\tfirst_failure_state.has_failed = False\n89\tfirst_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\n90\tfirst_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n91\t# Keep total_failures for historical context, or reset to 0\n92\t```\n93\t\n94\t### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification\n95\t\n96\t**IMPORTANT:** The state does NOT automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification. This prevents notification spam if failures continue. The state persists until application restart.\n97\t\n98\t## Thread-Safety Considerations\n99\t\n100\tSince adc is an async FastAPI application, multiple Telegram sends may execute concurrently. The state update MUST be atomic:\n101\t\n102\t```python\n103\t# Pattern: Check-and-set in one operation\n104\tif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n105\t    # This entire block should be protected by a lock if running in threaded context\n106\t    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n107\t    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n108\t    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n109\t    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n110\t    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)\n111\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n112\t    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n113\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n114\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n115\telse:\n116\t    # Subsequent failures — just increment counter\n117\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n118\t    log_failure(error, channel_id)  # No notification\n119\t```\n120\t\n121\t## Implementation Location\n122\t\n123\tThe state instance should live at module level in `src/monitoring/ambient.py`:\n124\t\n125\t```python\n126\t# src/monitoring/ambient.py\n127\tfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\n128\tfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\n129\tfrom typing import Optional\n130\t\n131\t@dataclass\n132\tclass FirstFailureState:\n133\t    # ... (as defined above)\n134\t\n135\t# Module-level singleton\n136\tfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n137\t```\n138\t\n139\tThis ensures:\n140\t1. Single source of truth across all ambient monitoring checks\n141\t2. State persists across requests (in-memory, per-process)\n142\t3. Reset on each uvicorn worker startup (expected behavior)\n143\t\n144\t## Usage Pattern\n145\t\n146\t```python\n147\t# In the Telegram send error handler\n148\tif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n149\t    # First failure! Record and notify\n150\t    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n151\t    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n152\t    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n153\t    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n154\t    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate if needed\n155\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n156\t    \n157\t    await send_first_failure_notification(\n158\t        channel_id=first_failure_state.channel_id,\n159\t        error_type=first_failure_state.error_type,\n160\t        error_message=first_failure_state.error_message,\n161\t        failed_at=first_failure_state.first_failure_at\n162\t    )\n163\t    \n164\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n165\t    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n166\telse:\n167\t    # Subsequent failure — just count it\n168\t    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n169\t    logger.warning(\n170\t        f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure (total: {first_failure_state.total_failures}): \"\n171\t        f\"{type(error).__name__}: {error}\"\n172\t    )\n173\t```\n174\t\n175\t## Data Validation\n176\t\n177\t- **`channel_id`** — Should be a valid Telegram channel ID string (e.g., `@channelname` or numeric ID)\n178\t- **`error_type`** — Should be the exception class name, not the full qualified path\n179\t- **`error_message`** — Consider truncating to ~500 chars to prevent excessive storage/logging\n180\t- **Timestamps** — Always use timezone-aware UTC datetimes\n181\t\n182\t## Future Extensions\n183\t\n184\tPotential enhancements not in v1:\n185\t\n186\t- **`last_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Track when the MOST RECENT failure occurred (for health checks)\n187\t- **`consecutive_failures: int`** — Track consecutive vs. total failures (reset on success)\n188\t- **`failure_history: list[FailureRecord]`** — Keep last N failures for pattern analysis\n189\t- **`recovery_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of first successful send after failure\n190\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"aa515eb0-0768-4fca-bcf5-373001a45b0e","uuid":"e6287687-d1b9-40e5-a8c9-62971ecfa67b","timestamp":"2026-07-19T23:22:28.613Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/docs/first-failure-state-structure.md","content":"# First-Failure State Data Structure\n\n## Overview\n\nThis document defines the data structure for tracking the first Telegram send failure after application startup. The state prevents duplicate notifications while maintaining diagnostic information.\n\n## State Structure\n\n```python\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    \"\"\"Tracks the first Telegram send failure after startup.\n    \n    This state is used to detect and report only the FIRST failure,\n    preventing notification spam on subsequent send failures.\n    \"\"\"\n    # Has the first failure been recorded?\n    has_failed: bool = False\n    \n    # When did the first failure occur?\n    first_failure_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n    \n    # Which channel failed?\n    channel_id: Optional[str] = None\n    \n    # What was the error?\n    error_type: Optional[str] = None  # e.g., \"TelegramError\", \"TimeoutError\"\n    error_message: Optional[str] = None\n    \n    # Total failure count (for diagnostics)\n    total_failures: int = 0\n    \n    # Have we sent the notification about this first failure?\n    notification_sent: bool = False\n    \n    # When did we send the notification?\n    notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime] = None\n```\n\n## Field Descriptions\n\n### Core State\n\n- **`has_failed: bool`** — Sentinel flag indicating whether ANY failure has occurred since startup. This is the primary field checked before triggering \"first failure\" logic.\n\n- **`first_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of the first failure occurrence. Set once on the first failure, never updated. Use UTC timezone-aware datetimes.\n\n- **`channel_id: Optional[str]`** — The Telegram channel ID that failed on the first occurrence. Useful for diagnosing whether a specific channel is problematic.\n\n### Error Information\n\n- **`error_type: Optional[str]`** — Exception class name (e.g., `\"TelegramError\"`, `\"ConnectionError\"`). Helps categorize the failure type.\n\n- **`error_message: Optional[str]`** — The error message or summary. Useful for quick diagnosis without digging through logs.\n\n### Diagnostics\n\n- **`total_failures: int`** — Monotonically increasing counter of ALL failures since startup. Helps assess failure frequency even after the first failure notification.\n\n### Notification Tracking\n\n- **`notification_sent: bool`** — Prevents duplicate notifications. Set to `True` after the first-failure alert is sent.\n\n- **`notification_sent_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of when the notification was sent. Useful for audit trails and debugging.\n\n## Reset Scenarios\n\n### 1. Application Startup (Automatic)\n\n```python\n# At module load time in src/monitoring/ambient.py\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n```\n\nAll fields default to their initial values (fresh state).\n\n### 2. Manual Reset (Admin Action)\n\nIf an admin manually resolves a Telegram issue and wants to reset failure tracking:\n\n```python\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()  # Fresh instance\n```\n\nOr selectively:\n\n```python\nfirst_failure_state.has_failed = False\nfirst_failure_state.first_failure_at = None\nfirst_failure_state.notification_sent = False\n# Keep total_failures for historical context, or reset to 0\n```\n\n### 3. No Auto-Reset After Notification\n\n**IMPORTANT:** The state does NOT automatically reset after sending the first-failure notification. This prevents notification spam if failures continue. The state persists until application restart.\n\n## Thread-Safety Considerations\n\nSince adc is an async FastAPI application, multiple Telegram sends may execute concurrently. The state update MUST be atomic:\n\n```python\n# Pattern: Check-and-set in one operation\nif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    # This entire block should be protected by a lock if running in threaded context\n    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    await send_first_failure_notification(...)\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\nelse:\n    # Subsequent failures — just increment counter\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    log_failure(error, channel_id)  # No notification\n```\n\n## Implementation Location\n\nThe state instance should live at module level in `src/monitoring/ambient.py`:\n\n```python\n# src/monitoring/ambient.py\nfrom dataclasses import dataclass, field\nfrom datetime import datetime, timezone\nfrom typing import Optional\n\n@dataclass\nclass FirstFailureState:\n    # ... (as defined above)\n\n# Module-level singleton\nfirst_failure_state = FirstFailureState()\n```\n\nThis ensures:\n1. Single source of truth across all ambient monitoring checks\n2. State persists across requests (in-memory, per-process)\n3. Reset on each uvicorn worker startup (expected behavior)\n\n## Usage Pattern\n\n```python\n# In the Telegram send error handler\nif not first_failure_state.has_failed:\n    # First failure! Record and notify\n    first_failure_state.has_failed = True\n    first_failure_state.first_failure_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\n    first_failure_state.channel_id = channel_id\n    first_failure_state.error_type = type(error).__name__\n    first_failure_state.error_message = str(error)[:500]  # Truncate if needed\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    \n    await send_first_failure_notification(\n        channel_id=first_failure_state.channel_id,\n        error_type=first_failure_state.error_type,\n        error_message=first_failure_state.error_message,\n        failed_at=first_failure_state.first_failure_at\n    )\n    \n    first_failure_state.notification_sent = True\n    first_failure_state.notification_sent_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc)\nelse:\n    # Subsequent failure — just count it\n    first_failure_state.total_failures += 1\n    logger.warning(\n        f\"Subsequent Telegram send failure (total: {first_failure_state.total_failures}): \"\n        f\"{type(error).__name__}: {error}\"\n    )\n```\n\n## Data Validation\n\n- **`channel_id`** — Should be a valid Telegram channel ID string (e.g., `@channelname` or numeric ID)\n- **`error_type`** — Should be the exception class name, not the full qualified path\n- **`error_message`** — Consider truncating to ~500 chars to prevent excessive storage/logging\n- **Timestamps** — Always use timezone-aware UTC datetimes\n\n## Future Extensions\n\nPotential enhancements not in v1:\n\n- **`last_failure_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Track when the MOST RECENT failure occurred (for health checks)\n- **`consecutive_failures: int`** — Track consecutive vs. total failures (reset on success)\n- **`failure_history: list[FailureRecord]`** — Keep last N failures for pattern analysis\n- **`recovery_at: Optional[datetime]`** — Timestamp of first successful send after failure\n","numLines":190,"startLine":1,"totalLines":190}}}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_fe29e8f1be0a40748f885bd5","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# First-Failure Tracking — Complete Design (End-to-End)\n2\t\n3\t**Bead:** adc-14la — \"Document complete first-failure tracking design\"\n4\t**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n5\t**Synthesizes:** adc-65l3 (data structure) + adc-2duz (storage) + adc-50ld (thread-safety,\n6\t**authoritative**) + adc-12bt (detection logic)\n7\t**Status:** Authoritative end-to-end design. This is the single document the implementation bead\n8\tshould implement against. **Where this document disagrees with the earlier synthesis\n9\t`notes/adc-4vhr-first-failure-tracking-design.md` (dated 2026-07-08), this document wins** —\n10\tthat earlier doc predates the adc-50ld / adc-12bt refinements and proposes a structure the\n11\tauthoritative child designs rejected (see §7, Reconciliation #1).\n12\t**Date:** 2026-07-19\n13\t\n14\t> **How to read this.** §1–§4 are the design itself (invariant, flow, state, code). §5 is\n15\t> concurrency in one screen. §6 is the edge-case catalog (compressed). §7 is the\n16\t> reconciliation table — the places the four child designs (and the old synthesis) disagreed,\n17\t> and which answer wins. §8 is the sequenced implementation plan for the next bead. Depth lives\n18\t> in the child docs; this document integrates them and resolves their conflicts.\n19\t\n20\t---\n21\t\n22\t## 1. The one invariant\n23\t\n24\t> **Exactly one first-failure notification is emitted per process startup** when the Telegram\n25\t> bridge first fails to accept a send. Every subsequent failure within that startup is silent\n26\t> (DEBUG + counters only).\n27\t\n28\tEverything else in this design exists to make that invariant hold under concurrency, hold\n29\twithout log spam, and hold robustly (not by accident). It is a **per-startup** semantic, not\n30\tper-deployment — a process restart re-arms detection intentionally (§6.5).\n31\t\n32\t---\n33\t\n34\t## 2. End-to-end flow\n35\t\n36\t```\n37\tProcess startup (FastAPI lifespan, src/main.py:152)\n38\t  │   get_telegram_fallback() → TelegramFallback() built on the serving loop\n39\t  │   __init__ sets: _has_logged_first_failure=False, _failure_count=0,\n40\t  │                  _first_failure_timestamp=None, _last_failure_timestamp=None,\n41\t  │                  _first_failure_lock=asyncio.Lock()\n42\t  │   (Separately: lifespan runs check_bridge_available() once → informs _is_reachable.\n43\t  │    This is a health-check concern, NOT the first-failure trigger — see adc-12bt §3.2.)\n44\t  ▼\n45\tsend_message() called (the only live entry to the failure path)\n46\t  │\n47\t  ├─ HTTP 200 ──► _is_reachable = True; return True          (no detection involvement)\n48\t  │\n49\t  └─ FAILURE (non-2xx | httpx.RequestError | other Exception)   ◄── reactive, AFTER a real failure\n50\t        │   await _handle_send_failure(error_context)             (adc-12bt §3: reactive-only)\n51\t        │\n52\t        ├─ async with self._first_failure_lock:                  (adc-50ld §5)\n53\t        │     was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)   # plain def, NO await\n54\t        │     │   inside: _is_reachable=False\n55\t        │     │           _failure_count += 1\n56\t        │     │           _last_failure_timestamp = now\n57\t        │     │           if not _has_logged_first_failure:   ← predicate\n58\t        │     │               _has_logged_first_failure = True        ← the claim (atomic w/ read)\n59\t        │     │               _first_failure_timestamp = now\n60\t        │     │               logger.warning(...)                     ← stdlib logging, no yield\n61\t        │     │               return True\n62\t        │     │           logger.debug(\"#%d ...\", _failure_count)\n63\t        │     │           return False\n64\t        │  ── lock released; was_first is final ──\n65\t        │\n66\t        └─ if was_first:\n67\t               await _notify_first_failure(error_context)   ← SIDE CHANNEL, outside the lock\n68\t                                                            (adc-12bt §7.4; never self.send_message)\n69\t  ▼\n70\tAll later failures: _has_logged_first_failure already True → _record_failure_locked\n71\treturns False → no notification, DEBUG + counter only. Suppression is stable for the\n72\trest of the process lifetime (no timer, no window).\n73\t  ▼\n74\tProcess restart → fresh singleton → all fields at defaults → next failure is \"first\" again.\n75\t```\n76\t\n77\t**Two structural facts the whole design rests on (adc-50ld §5.5):**\n78\t\n79\t1. **The decision (`was_first`) is captured inside the lock; the notification I/O runs after\n80\t   release.** This is what makes the exactly-once guarantee survive even if the notification is\n81\t   slow or fails (§6.6).\n82\t2. **`_record_failure_locked` is a plain `def` with no `await`.** Detection must not yield\n83\t   between the read of the flag and the write that flips it; the plain-`def` helper makes\n84\t   yielding mechanically impossible.\n85\t\n86\t---\n87\t\n88\t## 3. Integrated state model\n89\t\n90\tState lives as **flat instance variables on the `TelegramFallback` singleton** (adc-2duz's\n91\tdecision: in-memory, per-process, per-startup, **no persistence**), accessed via\n92\t`get_telegram_fallback()`.\n93\t\n94\t| Field | Type | Init | Written by | Semantics | Source |\n95\t|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n96\t| `_has_logged_first_failure` | `bool` | `False` | `_record_failure_locked` | The check-then-act flag. Monotonic `False→True` within a startup; the failure that flips it is \"first.\" | adc-65l3 |\n97\t| `_failure_count` | `int` | `0` | `_record_failure_locked` (unconditional `+= 1`) | Total failures since startup. Diagnostic; visible at the status endpoint. | adc-65l3 |\n98\t| `_first_failure_timestamp` | `datetime \\| None` | `None` | `_record_failure_locked` (set-once) | When the first failure occurred. \"Down since X.\" **New field** (current code lacks it). | adc-65l3 |\n99\t| `_last_failure_timestamp` | `datetime \\| None` | `None` | `_record_failure_locked` (every failure) | Most recent failure. \"Last failed Xs ago.\" **Rename** of current `_last_failure_logged` (which was updated only on the first failure — a latent bug). | adc-65l3 |\n100\t| `_first_failure_lock` | `asyncio.Lock` | `asyncio.Lock()` | (guard) | Serializes the critical section. Instance-level, created in `__init__`. | adc-50ld |\n101\t\n102\t**Explicitly out of scope of the lock:** `_is_reachable` (adc-50ld §5.4). It is written from\n103\t*different* paths (send success at `:80`, health-check at `:176/:179`, failure at `:207`), each\n104\ta single atomic `STORE_ATTR` with no check-then-act. Coupling those paths to a failure-path\n105\tlock would be wrong. The failure-path write of `_is_reachable = False` stays inside\n106\t`_record_failure_locked` for locality — that does not pull the *other* writers under the lock.\n107\tRule of thumb: **one lock per logical state object.**\n108\t\n109\t---\n110\t\n111\t## 4. Reference implementation (what the next bead writes)\n112\t\n113\tThis is the canonical, reconciled code — the four child designs collapsed into one. Every line\n114\tis annotated with the bead that owns the decision.\n115\t\n116\t### 4.1 `__init__` (adc-2duz storage + adc-65l3 fields + adc-50ld lock)\n117\t\n118\t```python\n119\tdef __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n120\t    import os\n121\t    self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n122\t        \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n123\t    )\n124\t\n125\t    # Reachability — separate logical object; its OTHER writers are NOT under the lock (adc-50ld §5.4)\n126\t    self._is_reachable = None  # None=unknown, True=reachable, False=unreachable\n127\t\n128\t    # First-failure record (adc-65l3 fields; adc-2duz: flat instance vars, no separate class)\n129\t    self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n130\t    self._failure_count: int = 0\n131\t    self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None   # NEW — set-once\n132\t    self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None    # RENAMED from _last_failure_logged\n133\t\n134\t    # Thread-safety (adc-50ld §5.1): one instance lock, await-free critical section\n135\t    self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n136\t```\n137\t\n138\t### 4.2 `send_message` failure branches become `await`ed (adc-12bt §3.3)\n139\t\n140\t```python\n141\t# Inside send_message — three failure branches, all now awaited (was: sync call):\n142\t#   non-2xx  → :84    await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n143\t#   RequestError → :89   await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n144\t#   other Exception → :93 await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n145\t```\n146\t\n147\t> ⚠️ **Sharp edge inherited unchanged (§6.3).** All three branches count as failure evidence\n148\t> today, including a 4xx (per-message error, not a bridge outage). v1 preserves current\n149\t> behavior; classifying 4xx out of the \"first failure\" trigger is a documented future change.\n150\t\n151\t### 4.3 The reactive handler + locked helper + side-channel notify\n152\t\n153\t```python\n154\tasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n155\t    \"\"\"Reactive detection entry point (adc-12bt §3). Called only from send_message failure branches.\"\"\"\n156\t    was_first = False\n157\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:                        # adc-50ld §5\n158\t        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)  # plain def; NO await (adc-50ld §5.5)\n159\t    # ---- lock released; the detection decision is final below ----\n160\t    if was_first:\n161\t        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)         # side-channel I/O (adc-12bt §7.4)\n162\t\n163\t\n164\tdef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n165\t    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await (adc-50ld §5.5).\n166\t\n167\t    Returns True iff THIS call performed the _has_logged_first_failure False→True flip,\n168\t    i.e. this is the first failure of the startup (adc-12bt §2). \"First\" = the winner of\n169\t    the claim, not a timestamp comparison.\n170\t    \"\"\"\n171\t    now = datetime.now()\n172\t    self._is_reachable = False                 # failure-path write; locality only (adc-50ld §5.4)\n173\t    self._failure_count += 1                   # unconditional, before the flag check (adc-12bt §5.2)\n174\t    self._last_failure_timestamp = now         # every failure (adc-65l3 rename)\n175\t\n176\t    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:     # the predicate\n177\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # the claim — atomic with the read, under the lock\n178\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = now    # set-once (adc-65l3)\n179\t        logger.warning(                        # stdlib logging: blocking I/O but does NOT yield (adc-50ld §3)\n180\t            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n181\t            f\"Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}. \"\n182\t            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n183\t        )\n184\t        return True                            # winner → triggers notification\n185\t    logger.debug(\n186\t        f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} \"\n187\t        f\"at {self.bridge_url}. Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}.\"\n188\t    )\n189\t    return False                               # loser → suppressed\n190\t\n191\t\n192\tasync def _notify_first_failure(self, error_context: str) -> None:\n193\t    \"\"\"Deliver the once-per-startup alert over a SIDE CHANNEL (adc-12bt §7.4).\n194\t\n195\t    MUST NOT call self.send_message(...): the bridge just failed for the same reason, and a\n196\t    failure here would pollute _failure_count / _last_failure_timestamp with self-failures.\n197\t    There is no infinite-recursion risk (the flag is already True, so any re-entry returns\n198\t    was_first=False), but the state pollution is real and prevented structurally by using a\n199\t    different channel. The exact channel (stderr / structured log sink / separate transport)\n200\t    is the notification-implementation bead's concern; v1 may ship this as a thin seam.\n201\t    \"\"\"\n202\t    # TODO(notification bead): choose the side channel. Left as a seam here.\n203\t    return\n204\t\n205\t\n206\tdef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n207\t    \"\"\"Lock-free read (adc-50ld §5.5 rule 6). Single-field atomic reads; monitoring tolerates\n208\t    staleness (Race 5).\"\"\"\n209\t    return {\n210\t        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n211\t        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n212\t        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n213\t        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n214\t        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n215\t            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n216\t        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n217\t            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n218\t    }\n219\t\n220\t\n221\tasync def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n222\t    \"\"\"Re-arm detection (adc-65l3 §3; adc-12bt §7.5). Used by future recovery-based reset,\n223\t    by any future hot-reload of bridge_url (§6.2), and as the test hook for re-arming.\n224\t    Counters are intentionally retained for diagnostics.\"\"\"\n225\t    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n226\t        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n227\t        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n228\t        # _failure_count and _last_failure_timestamp intentionally retained\n229\t```\n230\t\n231\t---\n232\t\n233\t## 5. Concurrency in one screen\n234\t\n235\t**Why the lock, given the current code is already safe?** CPython asyncio runs one task at a\n236\ttime and switches only at an `await` that yields. The *current* `_handle_send_failure` is\n237\tsynchronous and await-free, so it is **already atomic** with respect to every other coroutine\n238\t— the duplicate-WARNING race is *latent, not active* (adc-50ld §3). We add the lock as\n239\t**defense-in-depth**: the moment a maintainer adds an `await` inside the critical section\n240\t(async logging handler, a DB persist, an inline notification), the incidental atomicity\n241\tevaporates *silently*. An explicit lock + the plain-`def` helper make correctness survive that\n242\tchange, at a measured ~0.32 µs of isolated overhead on a path that is dormant today\n243\t(adc-50ld §6; adc-4rh3).\n244\t\n245\t**Under N near-simultaneous failures** (e.g. a burst of dispatches hitting a dead bridge),\n246\tall N coroutines call `_handle_send_failure`:\n247\t\n248\t- The lock serializes entry to `_record_failure_locked`.\n249\t- Coroutine 1 observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`, flips it, stamps\n250\t  `_first_failure_timestamp`, returns `was_first = True` → **it is \"the first.\"**\n251\t- Coroutines 2…N enter serially, observe `True`, increment the counter, return `False`.\n252\t- Net: **exactly one notification**, `_failure_count == N`, one `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n253\t\n254\t\"First\" is a **claim-and-set**, not a timestamp comparison — which is what makes it well-defined\n255\tunder concurrency and exactly-once without deduplication (adc-12bt §2, §4).\n256\t\n257\t**Reviewer-enforced structural rules (adc-50ld §5.5):**\n258\t1. One `asyncio.Lock` per instance, created in `__init__`.\n259\t2. No `await` inside the critical section — enforced by the plain-`def` helper.\n260\t3. I/O runs after release, keyed on `was_first`.\n261\t4. Never nest two `async with self._first_failure_lock:` (non-reentrant → deadlock).\n262\t5. No lock timeout (await-free section can only be held for microseconds).\n263\t6. `get_bridge_status` and all `_is_reachable` writes stay lock-free.\n264\t\n265\t---\n266\t\n267\t## 6. Edge cases (compressed — full detail in adc-12bt §7 / adc-50ld §7)\n268\t\n269\t| # | Case | Resolution |\n270\t|---|---|---|\n271\t| 6.1 | **Intermittent / flapping bridge** | Exactly one notification for the whole startup, at onset. Ongoing flap severity is still visible via `_failure_count` (climbing) and `_last_failure_timestamp` (recent) on the status endpoint. Re-alerting on each flap is deliberately *not* done; the extension point is recovery-based reset (§6.4), not a time cooldown. |\n272\t| 6.2 | **Config change (`ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL`)** | Read once in `__init__`; singleton lives for the process, so a change has no effect until restart — and restart resets the flag, so detection and config stay consistent *by accident of the singleton lifecycle*. **Rule for any future hot-reload:** any path that mutates `bridge_url` on a live instance MUST also call `reset_first_failure_state()`, or the new URL's first failure is suppressed by a stale flag. Prefer recreating the singleton over in-place mutation. |\n273\t| 6.3 | **4xx vs 5xx vs transport (sharp edge)** | Today all three failure branches flip the flag identically, so a single 400 (per-message error) can \"use up\" the one notification and suppress a later real outage. v1 keeps current behavior but documents it at the call site. **Future:** scope \"first failure\" to reachability-class failures (`httpx.RequestError` + 5xx/429); route 4xx to a per-message DEBUG path that does not touch the flag. |\n274\t| 6.4 | **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successes, `_handle_send_success` flips the flag back to `False`, re-arming detection so the *next* degradation is a new \"first.\" Requires adding `_consecutive_success_count` + threshold. Out of scope for v1. |\n275\t| 6.5 | **Restart resets everything** | Intentional. First-failure detection is a per-startup diagnostic. Each restart gets a fresh \"is the bridge down right now?\" window. If the bridge stays down across restarts, each restart correctly logs one WARNING. |\n276\t| 6.6 | **Notification failure does not un-set the flag** | The flag is set inside the lock, before release; `_notify_first_failure` runs after. If the notify raises/is cancelled/times out, the flag stays `True` and the next failure is \"subsequent\" — no re-notify. Desired exactly-once property, with the cost that a lost first notification is gone until reset/restart. Notify-retry, if ever needed, is a notification-layer concern and must NOT un-set the flag. |\n277\t| 6.7 | **Concurrent first failures** | The lock makes the claim atomic; exactly one of N wins. Detection defines *winning* (the flip); the lock is the *mechanism*. |\n278\t| 6.8 | **Dead constant** | `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300` (`fallback.py:34`) is declared but never referenced — a leftover from a superseded \"re-notify after 5 min\" idea. **Recommendation: delete it** in the implementation bead (or implement time-based re-notification deliberately, against `_last_failure_timestamp`, and remove this note). Do not leave a dead constant implying behavior the code does not have. |\n279\t\n280\t---\n281\t\n282\t## 7. Reconciliation — where the source designs disagreed, and which wins\n283\t\n284\tThis is the synthesis value-add. The four child designs were written over time (adc-65l3 /\n285\tadc-2duz on 2026-07-08; adc-50ld / adc-12bt on 2026-07-19), and the earlier synthesis\n286\t(`adc-4vhr-first-failure-tracking-design.md`) predates the refinements. Conflicts and winners:\n287\t\n288\t| # | Conflict | Older position | Authoritative position (wins) | Why |\n289\t|---|---|---|---|---|\n290\t| 1 | **Separate `FirstFailureTracker` class / `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py`?** | Old 4vhr synthesis proposed a dedicated tracker class returning log-level *strings* (`'WARNING'`/`'DEBUG'`). | **No.** Flat instance variables on `TelegramFallback`, no new module. | adc-65l3 and adc-2duz (the data-structure and storage child designs, which the old synthesis was summarizing) both specify flat instance vars. The singleton owns the state; a wrapper class adds indirection without encapsulation gain. |\n291\t| 2 | **Return value of the locked helper** | Old synthesis: a `'WARNING'`/`'DEBUG'` log-level string. | **A `was_first: bool`.** | adc-12bt §5.2 / adc-50ld §5.5. The helper's job is the claim, returning whether *this call* won. The WARNING log itself stays inside the helper (sync stdlib logging, no yield). The bool drives the post-lock side-channel notify. |\n292\t| 3 | **Where does the WARNING log live?** | Implicitly \"after the lock returns a level string.\" | **Inside `_record_failure_locked`** (under the lock); the *side-channel notification* runs after release. | adc-50ld §3: stdlib logging is blocking I/O but does not yield, so it is safe inside the await-free section. The structurally-expensive I/O (`_notify_first_failure`) is what must run after release. Two different things; the old synthesis conflated them. |\n293\t| 4 | **`_failure_count` update** | adc-65l3 sketch: `_failure_count = 1` in the first branch, `+= 1` in the else — redundant given the unconditional increment already at the top. | **Unconditional `_failure_count += 1` before the flag check** (one place). | adc-12bt §5.2. Removes the redundant branch-local write; counter is correct regardless of first/subsequent. |\n294\t| 5 | **Field naming** | Current code: `_last_failure_logged`, updated *only on the first failure*. | **`_last_failure_timestamp`, updated every failure; ADD `_first_failure_timestamp` (set-once).** | adc-65l3. The current name+behavior conflates \"last\" with \"first.\" Implementation aligns names when it lands. |\n295\t| 6 | **`_is_reachable` under the lock?** | Not addressed consistently. | **No** — its other writers (success, health-check) stay lock-free; only the failure-path write happens to live in the locked helper for locality. | adc-50ld §5.4: one lock per logical state object. |\n296\t| 7 | **\"Is a lock even needed?\"** | Old synthesis: yes, to fix an active race. adc-65l3/adc-2duz: lock to \"prevent race conditions.\" | **Defense-in-depth, not a bug fix.** The current await-free handler is already race-free on a single loop; the race is *latent*. | adc-50ld §3. Correct framing prevents over-engineering and records *why* the lock is cheap to take. |\n297\t| 8 | **`asyncio.Lock` rationale** | Earlier adc-50ld draft: \"`asyncio.Lock` because atomic operations are impossible in Python.\" | **`asyncio.Lock` because this state is in-memory, per-process, per-startup** (match the mechanism to where the state lives). | adc-50ld §5.3 corrects the prior draft. GIL atomics, SQLite txns, and `os.replace` all exist; \"atomics impossible\" is false. Right mechanism for a persisted/cross-worker variant is the SQLite-atomic CAS (§9). |\n298\t\n299\t---\n300\t\n301\t## 8. Implementation plan for the next bead\n302\t\n303\tThe implementation bead lands the §4 reference code in `src/telegram/fallback.py`. Sequenced so\n304\teach step leaves the tree green.\n305\t\n306\t### 8.1 Steps (in order)\n307\t\n308\t1. **Fields + lock in `__init__`.** Add `_first_failure_timestamp = None`; rename\n309\t   `_last_failure_logged` → `_last_failure_timestamp`; add\n310\t   `self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()`. Add `import asyncio` and keep\n311\t   `from datetime import datetime`, `from typing import Optional`.\n312\t2. **Split the handler.** Rename the body of the current sync `_handle_send_failure` into a new\n313\t   plain-`def _record_failure_locked(self, error_context) -> bool` that returns `was_first`,\n314\t   applying §4.3 (unconditional counter increment first; set-once first timestamp; return\n315\t   `True`/`False`). Make the new `_handle_send_failure` an `async def` that acquires the lock,\n316\t   calls the helper, and (if `was_first`) `await`s `_notify_first_failure`.\n317\t3. **Add the seam.** Add `async def _notify_first_failure(self, error_context)` as a documented\n318\t   no-op/`return` stub (§4.3) — the channel choice is a separate bead.\n319\t4. **Update the three call sites** in `send_message` (`:84`, `:89`, `:93`) to\n320\t   `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`.\n321\t5. **Extend `get_bridge_status`** with `has_logged_first_failure`, `first_failure_timestamp`,\n322\t   `last_failure_timestamp` (§4.3). Confirm the status endpoint at `src/main.py:~1469` still\n323\t   serializes cleanly.\n324\t6. **Add `reset_first_failure_state`** (§4.3) — needed by tests and by §6.2/§6.4 future hooks.\n325\t7. **Delete the dead constant** `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS` (§6.8) unless time-based\n326\t   re-notification is being implemented in the same bead.\n327\t\n328\t### 8.2 Tests to add (adc-50ld §9 / adc-5xuy §10)\n329\t\n330\t- **Concurrent first-failure:** `asyncio.gather` of N `_handle_send_failure` → exactly one\n331\t  WARNING, `_failure_count == N`, single `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n332\t- **Counter accuracy:** 100 concurrent calls → `_failure_count == 100`.\n333\t- **Structural no-I/O guard (load-bearing):**\n334\t  `assert inspect.iscoroutinefunction(fallback._record_failure_locked) is False`.\n335\t- **Subsequent suppression:** after the first failure, further failures emit DEBUG, no WARNING,\n336\t  and do not change `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n337\t- **Reset re-arms:** after `reset_first_failure_state()`, the next failure is \"first\" again;\n338\t  `_failure_count` is unchanged across the reset.\n339\t- **Read-doesn't-block:** `get_bridge_status()` returns promptly while a holder sleeps inside\n340\t  the (hypothetical) locked section.\n341\t\n342\t### 8.3 Verification\n343\t\n344\t- `.venv/bin/pytest` (the full suite; new tests collected).\n345\t- `.venv/bin/ruff check src/telegram/fallback.py`.\n346\t- Boot the server (`nohup .venv/bin/python -m uvicorn src.main:app …`) and confirm\n347\t  `/api/v1/status/telegram_bridge` returns the new fields; `/health` is still 200.\n348\t\n349\t### 8.4 What NOT to do\n350\t\n351\t- Do **not** create `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py` or a `FirstFailureTracker` class\n352\t  (Reconciliation #1).\n353\t- Do **not** put an `await` inside `_record_failure_locked` — that is the load-bearing\n354\t  invariant; the plain `def` enforces it (Reconciliation #2/#3, adc-50ld §5.5 rule 2).\n355\t- Do **not** make `_notify_first_failure` call `self.send_message(...)` (§6, adc-12bt §7.4).\n356\t- Do **not** pull `_is_reachable`'s other writers under the lock (Reconciliation #6).\n357\t- Do **not** add a lock timeout (adc-50ld §5.5 rule 5).\n358\t\n359\t---\n360\t\n361\t## 9. Upgrade path (when the design should change shape)\n362\t\n363\tSwitch the record to a **SQLite-atomic compare-and-set** (`UPDATE … SET claimed=1,\n364\tfirst_failure_at=? WHERE id=1 AND claimed=0` + `cur.rowcount == 1`; `aiosqlite` is already a\n365\tdependency, `src/session/store.py` already runs `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`) — **if any of these\n366\tbecomes true** (adc-50ld §8):\n367\t\n368\t- State must survive restarts (one first-failure per *deployment*, not per startup).\n369\t- adc is scaled to `uvicorn --workers N`.\n370\t- A `def` route or threadpool path starts touching this state.\n371\t\n372\tThis is a correctness/durability escalation (a SQL round-trip is ~0.1–1 ms vs ~0.32 µs for the\n373\tlock), **not** a performance one. Recorded here so it need not be re-derived.\n374\t\n375\t---\n376\t\n377\t## 10. Acceptance-criteria mapping\n378\t\n379\t| Criterion (adc-14la) | Where addressed |\n380\t|---|---|\n381\t| Complete design documented in this bead body | This document |\n382\t| All components integrated coherently | §2 (flow), §3 (state), §4 (code) integrate data-structure + storage + thread-safety + detection |\n383\t| Clear implementation guidance for next phase | §8 (sequenced plan, tests, verification, anti-patterns) |\n384\t| Depends on adc-12bt completing detection logic design | adc-12bt is **closed**; its detection logic is consumed in §2, §4.3, §6 |\n385\t\n386\t---\n387\t\n388\t## 11. References\n389\t\n390\t- **Data structure:** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md` — field set, types,\n391\t  reset/recovery semantics.\n392\t- **Storage:** `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` — flat instance vars on the singleton,\n393\t  in-memory per-startup, no persistence; options rejected.\n394\t- **Thread-safety (authoritative):** `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` — the\n395\t  `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked` pattern, latent-vs-active race framing,\n396\t  corrected performance figures.\n397\t  - Comprehensive spec: `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (concrete diff, reviewer\n398\t    checklist). Race catalog: `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`. Performance:\n399\t    `notes/adc-4rh3.md` (benchmark at `~/scratch/adc-4rh3-lock-bench.py`).\n400\t- **Detection logic:** `notes/adc-12bt-first-failure-detection-logic.md` — reactive-only\n401\t  timing, claim-and-set definition of \"first,\" win/lose under concurrency, edge cases.\n402\t- **Superseded synthesis:** `notes/adc-4vhr-first-failure-tracking-design.md` (2026-07-08) —\n403\t  predates adc-50ld/adc-12bt; its `FirstFailureTracker`-class proposal is rejected here (§7 #1).\n404\t- **Current code:** `src/telegram/fallback.py` — `_handle_send_failure` at `:198`, the three\n405\t  failure branches at `:84/:89/:93`, `__init__` at `:36`, dead `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS`\n406\t  at `:34`; singleton wired in `src/main.py:152`; status endpoint at `src/main.py:~1469`.\n407\t  Deployment is single-worker (`uvicorn src.main:app`, no `--workers`).\n408\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"aa515eb0-0768-4fca-bcf5-373001a45b0e","uuid":"6b941e94-95af-4cdc-9cd9-57becd57d9b9","timestamp":"2026-07-19T23:22:29.563Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/aide-de-camp/notes/adc-14la-first-failure-tracking-design.md","content":"# First-Failure Tracking — Complete Design (End-to-End)\n\n**Bead:** adc-14la — \"Document complete first-failure tracking design\"\n**Child of:** adc-4vhr (Design first-failure tracking mechanism)\n**Synthesizes:** adc-65l3 (data structure) + adc-2duz (storage) + adc-50ld (thread-safety,\n**authoritative**) + adc-12bt (detection logic)\n**Status:** Authoritative end-to-end design. This is the single document the implementation bead\nshould implement against. **Where this document disagrees with the earlier synthesis\n`notes/adc-4vhr-first-failure-tracking-design.md` (dated 2026-07-08), this document wins** —\nthat earlier doc predates the adc-50ld / adc-12bt refinements and proposes a structure the\nauthoritative child designs rejected (see §7, Reconciliation #1).\n**Date:** 2026-07-19\n\n> **How to read this.** §1–§4 are the design itself (invariant, flow, state, code). §5 is\n> concurrency in one screen. §6 is the edge-case catalog (compressed). §7 is the\n> reconciliation table — the places the four child designs (and the old synthesis) disagreed,\n> and which answer wins. §8 is the sequenced implementation plan for the next bead. Depth lives\n> in the child docs; this document integrates them and resolves their conflicts.\n\n---\n\n## 1. The one invariant\n\n> **Exactly one first-failure notification is emitted per process startup** when the Telegram\n> bridge first fails to accept a send. Every subsequent failure within that startup is silent\n> (DEBUG + counters only).\n\nEverything else in this design exists to make that invariant hold under concurrency, hold\nwithout log spam, and hold robustly (not by accident). It is a **per-startup** semantic, not\nper-deployment — a process restart re-arms detection intentionally (§6.5).\n\n---\n\n## 2. End-to-end flow\n\n```\nProcess startup (FastAPI lifespan, src/main.py:152)\n  │   get_telegram_fallback() → TelegramFallback() built on the serving loop\n  │   __init__ sets: _has_logged_first_failure=False, _failure_count=0,\n  │                  _first_failure_timestamp=None, _last_failure_timestamp=None,\n  │                  _first_failure_lock=asyncio.Lock()\n  │   (Separately: lifespan runs check_bridge_available() once → informs _is_reachable.\n  │    This is a health-check concern, NOT the first-failure trigger — see adc-12bt §3.2.)\n  ▼\nsend_message() called (the only live entry to the failure path)\n  │\n  ├─ HTTP 200 ──► _is_reachable = True; return True          (no detection involvement)\n  │\n  └─ FAILURE (non-2xx | httpx.RequestError | other Exception)   ◄── reactive, AFTER a real failure\n        │   await _handle_send_failure(error_context)             (adc-12bt §3: reactive-only)\n        │\n        ├─ async with self._first_failure_lock:                  (adc-50ld §5)\n        │     was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)   # plain def, NO await\n        │     │   inside: _is_reachable=False\n        │     │           _failure_count += 1\n        │     │           _last_failure_timestamp = now\n        │     │           if not _has_logged_first_failure:   ← predicate\n        │     │               _has_logged_first_failure = True        ← the claim (atomic w/ read)\n        │     │               _first_failure_timestamp = now\n        │     │               logger.warning(...)                     ← stdlib logging, no yield\n        │     │               return True\n        │     │           logger.debug(\"#%d ...\", _failure_count)\n        │     │           return False\n        │  ── lock released; was_first is final ──\n        │\n        └─ if was_first:\n               await _notify_first_failure(error_context)   ← SIDE CHANNEL, outside the lock\n                                                            (adc-12bt §7.4; never self.send_message)\n  ▼\nAll later failures: _has_logged_first_failure already True → _record_failure_locked\nreturns False → no notification, DEBUG + counter only. Suppression is stable for the\nrest of the process lifetime (no timer, no window).\n  ▼\nProcess restart → fresh singleton → all fields at defaults → next failure is \"first\" again.\n```\n\n**Two structural facts the whole design rests on (adc-50ld §5.5):**\n\n1. **The decision (`was_first`) is captured inside the lock; the notification I/O runs after\n   release.** This is what makes the exactly-once guarantee survive even if the notification is\n   slow or fails (§6.6).\n2. **`_record_failure_locked` is a plain `def` with no `await`.** Detection must not yield\n   between the read of the flag and the write that flips it; the plain-`def` helper makes\n   yielding mechanically impossible.\n\n---\n\n## 3. Integrated state model\n\nState lives as **flat instance variables on the `TelegramFallback` singleton** (adc-2duz's\ndecision: in-memory, per-process, per-startup, **no persistence**), accessed via\n`get_telegram_fallback()`.\n\n| Field | Type | Init | Written by | Semantics | Source |\n|---|---|---|---|---|---|\n| `_has_logged_first_failure` | `bool` | `False` | `_record_failure_locked` | The check-then-act flag. Monotonic `False→True` within a startup; the failure that flips it is \"first.\" | adc-65l3 |\n| `_failure_count` | `int` | `0` | `_record_failure_locked` (unconditional `+= 1`) | Total failures since startup. Diagnostic; visible at the status endpoint. | adc-65l3 |\n| `_first_failure_timestamp` | `datetime \\| None` | `None` | `_record_failure_locked` (set-once) | When the first failure occurred. \"Down since X.\" **New field** (current code lacks it). | adc-65l3 |\n| `_last_failure_timestamp` | `datetime \\| None` | `None` | `_record_failure_locked` (every failure) | Most recent failure. \"Last failed Xs ago.\" **Rename** of current `_last_failure_logged` (which was updated only on the first failure — a latent bug). | adc-65l3 |\n| `_first_failure_lock` | `asyncio.Lock` | `asyncio.Lock()` | (guard) | Serializes the critical section. Instance-level, created in `__init__`. | adc-50ld |\n\n**Explicitly out of scope of the lock:** `_is_reachable` (adc-50ld §5.4). It is written from\n*different* paths (send success at `:80`, health-check at `:176/:179`, failure at `:207`), each\na single atomic `STORE_ATTR` with no check-then-act. Coupling those paths to a failure-path\nlock would be wrong. The failure-path write of `_is_reachable = False` stays inside\n`_record_failure_locked` for locality — that does not pull the *other* writers under the lock.\nRule of thumb: **one lock per logical state object.**\n\n---\n\n## 4. Reference implementation (what the next bead writes)\n\nThis is the canonical, reconciled code — the four child designs collapsed into one. Every line\nis annotated with the bead that owns the decision.\n\n### 4.1 `__init__` (adc-2duz storage + adc-65l3 fields + adc-50ld lock)\n\n```python\ndef __init__(self, bridge_url: str | None = None):\n    import os\n    self.bridge_url = bridge_url or os.getenv(\n        \"ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL\", self.DEFAULT_BRIDGE_URL\n    )\n\n    # Reachability — separate logical object; its OTHER writers are NOT under the lock (adc-50ld §5.4)\n    self._is_reachable = None  # None=unknown, True=reachable, False=unreachable\n\n    # First-failure record (adc-65l3 fields; adc-2duz: flat instance vars, no separate class)\n    self._has_logged_first_failure: bool = False\n    self._failure_count: int = 0\n    self._first_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None   # NEW — set-once\n    self._last_failure_timestamp: Optional[datetime] = None    # RENAMED from _last_failure_logged\n\n    # Thread-safety (adc-50ld §5.1): one instance lock, await-free critical section\n    self._first_failure_lock: asyncio.Lock = asyncio.Lock()\n```\n\n### 4.2 `send_message` failure branches become `await`ed (adc-12bt §3.3)\n\n```python\n# Inside send_message — three failure branches, all now awaited (was: sync call):\n#   non-2xx  → :84    await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n#   RequestError → :89   await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n#   other Exception → :93 await self._handle_send_failure(error_msg)\n```\n\n> ⚠️ **Sharp edge inherited unchanged (§6.3).** All three branches count as failure evidence\n> today, including a 4xx (per-message error, not a bridge outage). v1 preserves current\n> behavior; classifying 4xx out of the \"first failure\" trigger is a documented future change.\n\n### 4.3 The reactive handler + locked helper + side-channel notify\n\n```python\nasync def _handle_send_failure(self, error_context: str = \"\") -> None:\n    \"\"\"Reactive detection entry point (adc-12bt §3). Called only from send_message failure branches.\"\"\"\n    was_first = False\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:                        # adc-50ld §5\n        was_first = self._record_failure_locked(error_context)  # plain def; NO await (adc-50ld §5.5)\n    # ---- lock released; the detection decision is final below ----\n    if was_first:\n        await self._notify_first_failure(error_context)         # side-channel I/O (adc-12bt §7.4)\n\n\ndef _record_failure_locked(self, error_context: str) -> bool:\n    \"\"\"Caller MUST hold _first_failure_lock. Sync on purpose — no await (adc-50ld §5.5).\n\n    Returns True iff THIS call performed the _has_logged_first_failure False→True flip,\n    i.e. this is the first failure of the startup (adc-12bt §2). \"First\" = the winner of\n    the claim, not a timestamp comparison.\n    \"\"\"\n    now = datetime.now()\n    self._is_reachable = False                 # failure-path write; locality only (adc-50ld §5.4)\n    self._failure_count += 1                   # unconditional, before the flag check (adc-12bt §5.2)\n    self._last_failure_timestamp = now         # every failure (adc-65l3 rename)\n\n    if not self._has_logged_first_failure:     # the predicate\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = True  # the claim — atomic with the read, under the lock\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = now    # set-once (adc-65l3)\n        logger.warning(                        # stdlib logging: blocking I/O but does NOT yield (adc-50ld §3)\n            f\"First Telegram send failure detected at {self.bridge_url}. \"\n            f\"Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}. \"\n            f\"Subsequent failures will be logged at DEBUG level only.\"\n        )\n        return True                            # winner → triggers notification\n    logger.debug(\n        f\"Repeated Telegram send failure #{self._failure_count} \"\n        f\"at {self.bridge_url}. Error: {error_context or 'unknown error'}.\"\n    )\n    return False                               # loser → suppressed\n\n\nasync def _notify_first_failure(self, error_context: str) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Deliver the once-per-startup alert over a SIDE CHANNEL (adc-12bt §7.4).\n\n    MUST NOT call self.send_message(...): the bridge just failed for the same reason, and a\n    failure here would pollute _failure_count / _last_failure_timestamp with self-failures.\n    There is no infinite-recursion risk (the flag is already True, so any re-entry returns\n    was_first=False), but the state pollution is real and prevented structurally by using a\n    different channel. The exact channel (stderr / structured log sink / separate transport)\n    is the notification-implementation bead's concern; v1 may ship this as a thin seam.\n    \"\"\"\n    # TODO(notification bead): choose the side channel. Left as a seam here.\n    return\n\n\ndef get_bridge_status(self) -> dict:\n    \"\"\"Lock-free read (adc-50ld §5.5 rule 6). Single-field atomic reads; monitoring tolerates\n    staleness (Race 5).\"\"\"\n    return {\n        \"reachable\": self._is_reachable,\n        \"bridge_url\": self.bridge_url,\n        \"failure_count\": self._failure_count,\n        \"has_logged_first_failure\": self._has_logged_first_failure,\n        \"first_failure_timestamp\": self._first_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n            if self._first_failure_timestamp else None,\n        \"last_failure_timestamp\": self._last_failure_timestamp.isoformat()\n            if self._last_failure_timestamp else None,\n    }\n\n\nasync def reset_first_failure_state(self) -> None:\n    \"\"\"Re-arm detection (adc-65l3 §3; adc-12bt §7.5). Used by future recovery-based reset,\n    by any future hot-reload of bridge_url (§6.2), and as the test hook for re-arming.\n    Counters are intentionally retained for diagnostics.\"\"\"\n    async with self._first_failure_lock:\n        self._has_logged_first_failure = False\n        self._first_failure_timestamp = None\n        # _failure_count and _last_failure_timestamp intentionally retained\n```\n\n---\n\n## 5. Concurrency in one screen\n\n**Why the lock, given the current code is already safe?** CPython asyncio runs one task at a\ntime and switches only at an `await` that yields. The *current* `_handle_send_failure` is\nsynchronous and await-free, so it is **already atomic** with respect to every other coroutine\n— the duplicate-WARNING race is *latent, not active* (adc-50ld §3). We add the lock as\n**defense-in-depth**: the moment a maintainer adds an `await` inside the critical section\n(async logging handler, a DB persist, an inline notification), the incidental atomicity\nevaporates *silently*. An explicit lock + the plain-`def` helper make correctness survive that\nchange, at a measured ~0.32 µs of isolated overhead on a path that is dormant today\n(adc-50ld §6; adc-4rh3).\n\n**Under N near-simultaneous failures** (e.g. a burst of dispatches hitting a dead bridge),\nall N coroutines call `_handle_send_failure`:\n\n- The lock serializes entry to `_record_failure_locked`.\n- Coroutine 1 observes `_has_logged_first_failure == False`, flips it, stamps\n  `_first_failure_timestamp`, returns `was_first = True` → **it is \"the first.\"**\n- Coroutines 2…N enter serially, observe `True`, increment the counter, return `False`.\n- Net: **exactly one notification**, `_failure_count == N`, one `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n\n\"First\" is a **claim-and-set**, not a timestamp comparison — which is what makes it well-defined\nunder concurrency and exactly-once without deduplication (adc-12bt §2, §4).\n\n**Reviewer-enforced structural rules (adc-50ld §5.5):**\n1. One `asyncio.Lock` per instance, created in `__init__`.\n2. No `await` inside the critical section — enforced by the plain-`def` helper.\n3. I/O runs after release, keyed on `was_first`.\n4. Never nest two `async with self._first_failure_lock:` (non-reentrant → deadlock).\n5. No lock timeout (await-free section can only be held for microseconds).\n6. `get_bridge_status` and all `_is_reachable` writes stay lock-free.\n\n---\n\n## 6. Edge cases (compressed — full detail in adc-12bt §7 / adc-50ld §7)\n\n| # | Case | Resolution |\n|---|---|---|\n| 6.1 | **Intermittent / flapping bridge** | Exactly one notification for the whole startup, at onset. Ongoing flap severity is still visible via `_failure_count` (climbing) and `_last_failure_timestamp` (recent) on the status endpoint. Re-alerting on each flap is deliberately *not* done; the extension point is recovery-based reset (§6.4), not a time cooldown. |\n| 6.2 | **Config change (`ADC_TELEGRAM_BRIDGE_URL`)** | Read once in `__init__`; singleton lives for the process, so a change has no effect until restart — and restart resets the flag, so detection and config stay consistent *by accident of the singleton lifecycle*. **Rule for any future hot-reload:** any path that mutates `bridge_url` on a live instance MUST also call `reset_first_failure_state()`, or the new URL's first failure is suppressed by a stale flag. Prefer recreating the singleton over in-place mutation. |\n| 6.3 | **4xx vs 5xx vs transport (sharp edge)** | Today all three failure branches flip the flag identically, so a single 400 (per-message error) can \"use up\" the one notification and suppress a later real outage. v1 keeps current behavior but documents it at the call site. **Future:** scope \"first failure\" to reachability-class failures (`httpx.RequestError` + 5xx/429); route 4xx to a per-message DEBUG path that does not touch the flag. |\n| 6.4 | **Recovery-based reset (future)** | After N consecutive successes, `_handle_send_success` flips the flag back to `False`, re-arming detection so the *next* degradation is a new \"first.\" Requires adding `_consecutive_success_count` + threshold. Out of scope for v1. |\n| 6.5 | **Restart resets everything** | Intentional. First-failure detection is a per-startup diagnostic. Each restart gets a fresh \"is the bridge down right now?\" window. If the bridge stays down across restarts, each restart correctly logs one WARNING. |\n| 6.6 | **Notification failure does not un-set the flag** | The flag is set inside the lock, before release; `_notify_first_failure` runs after. If the notify raises/is cancelled/times out, the flag stays `True` and the next failure is \"subsequent\" — no re-notify. Desired exactly-once property, with the cost that a lost first notification is gone until reset/restart. Notify-retry, if ever needed, is a notification-layer concern and must NOT un-set the flag. |\n| 6.7 | **Concurrent first failures** | The lock makes the claim atomic; exactly one of N wins. Detection defines *winning* (the flip); the lock is the *mechanism*. |\n| 6.8 | **Dead constant** | `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS = 300` (`fallback.py:34`) is declared but never referenced — a leftover from a superseded \"re-notify after 5 min\" idea. **Recommendation: delete it** in the implementation bead (or implement time-based re-notification deliberately, against `_last_failure_timestamp`, and remove this note). Do not leave a dead constant implying behavior the code does not have. |\n\n---\n\n## 7. Reconciliation — where the source designs disagreed, and which wins\n\nThis is the synthesis value-add. The four child designs were written over time (adc-65l3 /\nadc-2duz on 2026-07-08; adc-50ld / adc-12bt on 2026-07-19), and the earlier synthesis\n(`adc-4vhr-first-failure-tracking-design.md`) predates the refinements. Conflicts and winners:\n\n| # | Conflict | Older position | Authoritative position (wins) | Why |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | **Separate `FirstFailureTracker` class / `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py`?** | Old 4vhr synthesis proposed a dedicated tracker class returning log-level *strings* (`'WARNING'`/`'DEBUG'`). | **No.** Flat instance variables on `TelegramFallback`, no new module. | adc-65l3 and adc-2duz (the data-structure and storage child designs, which the old synthesis was summarizing) both specify flat instance vars. The singleton owns the state; a wrapper class adds indirection without encapsulation gain. |\n| 2 | **Return value of the locked helper** | Old synthesis: a `'WARNING'`/`'DEBUG'` log-level string. | **A `was_first: bool`.** | adc-12bt §5.2 / adc-50ld §5.5. The helper's job is the claim, returning whether *this call* won. The WARNING log itself stays inside the helper (sync stdlib logging, no yield). The bool drives the post-lock side-channel notify. |\n| 3 | **Where does the WARNING log live?** | Implicitly \"after the lock returns a level string.\" | **Inside `_record_failure_locked`** (under the lock); the *side-channel notification* runs after release. | adc-50ld §3: stdlib logging is blocking I/O but does not yield, so it is safe inside the await-free section. The structurally-expensive I/O (`_notify_first_failure`) is what must run after release. Two different things; the old synthesis conflated them. |\n| 4 | **`_failure_count` update** | adc-65l3 sketch: `_failure_count = 1` in the first branch, `+= 1` in the else — redundant given the unconditional increment already at the top. | **Unconditional `_failure_count += 1` before the flag check** (one place). | adc-12bt §5.2. Removes the redundant branch-local write; counter is correct regardless of first/subsequent. |\n| 5 | **Field naming** | Current code: `_last_failure_logged`, updated *only on the first failure*. | **`_last_failure_timestamp`, updated every failure; ADD `_first_failure_timestamp` (set-once).** | adc-65l3. The current name+behavior conflates \"last\" with \"first.\" Implementation aligns names when it lands. |\n| 6 | **`_is_reachable` under the lock?** | Not addressed consistently. | **No** — its other writers (success, health-check) stay lock-free; only the failure-path write happens to live in the locked helper for locality. | adc-50ld §5.4: one lock per logical state object. |\n| 7 | **\"Is a lock even needed?\"** | Old synthesis: yes, to fix an active race. adc-65l3/adc-2duz: lock to \"prevent race conditions.\" | **Defense-in-depth, not a bug fix.** The current await-free handler is already race-free on a single loop; the race is *latent*. | adc-50ld §3. Correct framing prevents over-engineering and records *why* the lock is cheap to take. |\n| 8 | **`asyncio.Lock` rationale** | Earlier adc-50ld draft: \"`asyncio.Lock` because atomic operations are impossible in Python.\" | **`asyncio.Lock` because this state is in-memory, per-process, per-startup** (match the mechanism to where the state lives). | adc-50ld §5.3 corrects the prior draft. GIL atomics, SQLite txns, and `os.replace` all exist; \"atomics impossible\" is false. Right mechanism for a persisted/cross-worker variant is the SQLite-atomic CAS (§9). |\n\n---\n\n## 8. Implementation plan for the next bead\n\nThe implementation bead lands the §4 reference code in `src/telegram/fallback.py`. Sequenced so\neach step leaves the tree green.\n\n### 8.1 Steps (in order)\n\n1. **Fields + lock in `__init__`.** Add `_first_failure_timestamp = None`; rename\n   `_last_failure_logged` → `_last_failure_timestamp`; add\n   `self._first_failure_lock = asyncio.Lock()`. Add `import asyncio` and keep\n   `from datetime import datetime`, `from typing import Optional`.\n2. **Split the handler.** Rename the body of the current sync `_handle_send_failure` into a new\n   plain-`def _record_failure_locked(self, error_context) -> bool` that returns `was_first`,\n   applying §4.3 (unconditional counter increment first; set-once first timestamp; return\n   `True`/`False`). Make the new `_handle_send_failure` an `async def` that acquires the lock,\n   calls the helper, and (if `was_first`) `await`s `_notify_first_failure`.\n3. **Add the seam.** Add `async def _notify_first_failure(self, error_context)` as a documented\n   no-op/`return` stub (§4.3) — the channel choice is a separate bead.\n4. **Update the three call sites** in `send_message` (`:84`, `:89`, `:93`) to\n   `await self._handle_send_failure(...)`.\n5. **Extend `get_bridge_status`** with `has_logged_first_failure`, `first_failure_timestamp`,\n   `last_failure_timestamp` (§4.3). Confirm the status endpoint at `src/main.py:~1469` still\n   serializes cleanly.\n6. **Add `reset_first_failure_state`** (§4.3) — needed by tests and by §6.2/§6.4 future hooks.\n7. **Delete the dead constant** `FAILURE_LOG_COOLDOWN_SECONDS` (§6.8) unless time-based\n   re-notification is being implemented in the same bead.\n\n### 8.2 Tests to add (adc-50ld §9 / adc-5xuy §10)\n\n- **Concurrent first-failure:** `asyncio.gather` of N `_handle_send_failure` → exactly one\n  WARNING, `_failure_count == N`, single `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n- **Counter accuracy:** 100 concurrent calls → `_failure_count == 100`.\n- **Structural no-I/O guard (load-bearing):**\n  `assert inspect.iscoroutinefunction(fallback._record_failure_locked) is False`.\n- **Subsequent suppression:** after the first failure, further failures emit DEBUG, no WARNING,\n  and do not change `_first_failure_timestamp`.\n- **Reset re-arms:** after `reset_first_failure_state()`, the next failure is \"first\" again;\n  `_failure_count` is unchanged across the reset.\n- **Read-doesn't-block:** `get_bridge_status()` returns promptly while a holder sleeps inside\n  the (hypothetical) locked section.\n\n### 8.3 Verification\n\n- `.venv/bin/pytest` (the full suite; new tests collected).\n- `.venv/bin/ruff check src/telegram/fallback.py`.\n- Boot the server (`nohup .venv/bin/python -m uvicorn src.main:app …`) and confirm\n  `/api/v1/status/telegram_bridge` returns the new fields; `/health` is still 200.\n\n### 8.4 What NOT to do\n\n- Do **not** create `src/telegram/first_failure_tracker.py` or a `FirstFailureTracker` class\n  (Reconciliation #1).\n- Do **not** put an `await` inside `_record_failure_locked` — that is the load-bearing\n  invariant; the plain `def` enforces it (Reconciliation #2/#3, adc-50ld §5.5 rule 2).\n- Do **not** make `_notify_first_failure` call `self.send_message(...)` (§6, adc-12bt §7.4).\n- Do **not** pull `_is_reachable`'s other writers under the lock (Reconciliation #6).\n- Do **not** add a lock timeout (adc-50ld §5.5 rule 5).\n\n---\n\n## 9. Upgrade path (when the design should change shape)\n\nSwitch the record to a **SQLite-atomic compare-and-set** (`UPDATE … SET claimed=1,\nfirst_failure_at=? WHERE id=1 AND claimed=0` + `cur.rowcount == 1`; `aiosqlite` is already a\ndependency, `src/session/store.py` already runs `PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL`) — **if any of these\nbecomes true** (adc-50ld §8):\n\n- State must survive restarts (one first-failure per *deployment*, not per startup).\n- adc is scaled to `uvicorn --workers N`.\n- A `def` route or threadpool path starts touching this state.\n\nThis is a correctness/durability escalation (a SQL round-trip is ~0.1–1 ms vs ~0.32 µs for the\nlock), **not** a performance one. Recorded here so it need not be re-derived.\n\n---\n\n## 10. Acceptance-criteria mapping\n\n| Criterion (adc-14la) | Where addressed |\n|---|---|\n| Complete design documented in this bead body | This document |\n| All components integrated coherently | §2 (flow), §3 (state), §4 (code) integrate data-structure + storage + thread-safety + detection |\n| Clear implementation guidance for next phase | §8 (sequenced plan, tests, verification, anti-patterns) |\n| Depends on adc-12bt completing detection logic design | adc-12bt is **closed**; its detection logic is consumed in §2, §4.3, §6 |\n\n---\n\n## 11. References\n\n- **Data structure:** `notes/adc-65l3-first-failure-state-structure.md` — field set, types,\n  reset/recovery semantics.\n- **Storage:** `notes/adc-2duz-state-storage-design.md` — flat instance vars on the singleton,\n  in-memory per-startup, no persistence; options rejected.\n- **Thread-safety (authoritative):** `notes/adc-50ld-thread-safety-approach.md` — the\n  `asyncio.Lock` + await-free `_record_failure_locked` pattern, latent-vs-active race framing,\n  corrected performance figures.\n  - Comprehensive spec: `notes/adc-5xuy-thread-safety-design.md` (concrete diff, reviewer\n    checklist). Race catalog: `notes/adc-4ol5-race-conditions.md`.
